content.body: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment despite changes in external conditions. The body must keep conditions within narrow limits for enzymes to work properly, cells to function, and chemical reactions to proceed at the correct rate.

Conditions regulated by homeostasis:
• Core body temperature: ~37°C (humans)
• Blood glucose concentration: ~80-120 mg/100mL
• Blood pH: ~7.4
• Water and ion balance (osmoregulation)
• CO₂ concentration in blood

Negative feedback: the mechanism of homeostasis
Deviation detected → corrective response → returns to set point
(Negative because the response opposes the change)

content.title: Thermoregulation — Body Temperature Control
content.body:
OVERHEATING responses (lower temperature):
• Sweating: evaporation of sweat cools skin
• Vasodilation: blood vessels in skin dilate → more blood to skin → more heat radiated
• Reduced metabolic rate
• Behaviour: moving to shade, removing clothing

COLD responses (raise temperature):
• Shivering: rapid muscle contractions generate heat
• Vasoconstriction: blood vessels in skin constrict → less blood to surface → less heat lost
• Erector pili muscles raise hairs → traps air insulation (more effective in animals with fur)
• Increased metabolic rate
• Behaviour: moving to warmth, adding clothing

Blood Glucose Regulation — Pancreatic hormones:
After eating (high blood glucose):
1. β-cells in islets of Langerhans detect high glucose
2. β-cells secrete INSULIN
3. Insulin stimulates cells to take up glucose
4. Liver converts glucose → glycogen (glycogenesis)
5. Blood glucose returns to normal

During fasting/exercise (low blood glucose):
1. α-cells detect low glucose
2. α-cells secrete GLUCAGON
3. Glucagon stimulates liver to convert glycogen → glucose (glycogenolysis)
4. Blood glucose rises back to normal

Diabetes Mellitus:
Type 1 (Insulin-dependent): β-cells destroyed (autoimmune). Body produces no insulin. Blood glucose rises dangerously. Treatment: insulin injections, blood glucose monitoring.

Type 2 (Non-insulin-dependent): cells become resistant to insulin (often linked to obesity, diet). Treatment: diet, exercise, oral medication. More common — increasingly affecting young people in Uganda and globally.

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains a constant:
questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the maintenance of a stable internal environment — keeping conditions like temperature and glucose within narrow limits.

questions.question: Negative feedback in homeostasis means:
options: The response makes the deviation worse
The response opposes the change and returns conditions to normal
questions.answer: The response opposes the change and returns conditions to normal
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: the response is in the OPPOSITE direction to the change — it corrects the deviation and restores the set point.

questions.question: In hot conditions, blood vessels in the skin:
questions.explanation: Vasodilation brings more blood to the skin surface, increasing heat radiation and cooling the body.

questions.question: Insulin is produced by:
questions.explanation: Beta (β) cells in the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas produce insulin when blood glucose rises.

questions.question: Insulin causes blood glucose to:
questions.explanation: Insulin stimulates cells to absorb glucose from blood and liver to store it as glycogen — lowering blood glucose.

questions.question: Glucagon is secreted when blood glucose is:
questions.explanation: Glucagon (from α-cells) is released when blood glucose falls too low — it stimulates glycogen breakdown to release glucose.

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes is caused by:
options: Autoimmune destruction of β-cells — no insulin produced
questions.answer: Autoimmune destruction of β-cells — no insulin produced
questions.explanation: In Type 1 diabetes, the immune system destroys the β-cells. The pancreas cannot produce insulin.

questions.question: Shivering in cold conditions generates heat by:
questions.explanation: Shivering is rapid, involuntary muscle contraction that generates metabolic heat to warm the body.

questions.question: Glycogenesis is:
questions.explanation: Glycogenesis: glucose → glycogen. Triggered by insulin when blood glucose is high. Glycogen is stored in the liver and muscles.

questions.question: Why is tight blood glucose control important?
options: Enzymes only work properly within a narrow glucose range
Blood needs exactly 80mg/100mL to be red
questions.answer: Enzymes only work properly within a narrow glucose range
questions.explanation: All cellular reactions are enzyme-controlled. Enzymes have optimal conditions. Extreme blood glucose disrupts osmosis, cell function, and eventually causes organ damage.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Reproduction is the production of new individuals from existing ones, ensuring the continuation of the species. Sexual reproduction (in humans) involves the fusion of male and female gametes (sex cells) — sperm and egg — in fertilisation to form a zygote.

Male reproductive system:
Testes: produce sperm (by meiosis) and testosterone
Epididymis: sperm mature and are stored
Vas deferens: tube carrying sperm
Seminal vesicle + prostate: add fluid → semen
Urethra + penis: deliver semen

Female reproductive system:
Ovaries: produce eggs (by meiosis) and oestrogen/progesterone
Fallopian tubes (oviducts): egg transport; site of fertilisation
Uterus (womb): site of implantation and fetal development
Cervix: lower part of uterus
Vagina: birth canal

The Menstrual Cycle (28 days):
Days 1-5: Menstruation (uterus lining shed)
Days 6-13: Follicular phase — FSH stimulates follicle development; oestrogen rises → lining thickens
Day 14: Ovulation — LH surge triggers egg release from ovary
Days 15-28: Luteal phase — corpus luteum produces progesterone → lining maintained
If no fertilisation: progesterone falls → lining shed → menstruation

content.title: Fertilisation and Development
content.body:
1. Sperm deposited in vagina, swim through cervix, uterus
2. Fertilisation occurs in fallopian tube: sperm fuses with egg → zygote
3. Zygote undergoes mitosis (cell division) as it travels to uterus
4. Implantation: blastocyst embeds in uterine wall (~day 6-10)
5. Placenta develops: supplies nutrients and O₂, removes CO₂ and waste from fetus
6. Amniotic fluid: surrounds and protects fetus
7. Umbilical cord: connects fetus to placenta
8. Birth ~9 months after fertilisation

Contraception methods:
Hormonal: pill (oestrogen/progesterone), injections, implant — prevent ovulation
Barrier: condom (also prevents STIs), diaphragm
Intrauterine: IUD — prevents implantation
Natural: calendar/rhythm method (least reliable)

Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) common in Uganda:
HIV/AIDS: virus attacking immune system (CD4 T-cells). No cure but ARVs allow normal life.
Gonorrhoea: bacterial — treated with antibiotics
Syphilis: bacterial — treated with penicillin
HPV: virus — can cause cervical cancer; vaccine available
Prevention: abstinence, mutual faithfulness, condom use (ABC approach)

questions.question: Where does fertilisation normally occur?
questions.explanation: Fertilisation (sperm + egg fusion) normally occurs in the fallopian tube (oviduct), not the uterus.

questions.question: Ovulation occurs on approximately which day of a 28-day cycle?
questions.explanation: Ovulation (egg release from ovary) is triggered by an LH surge around day 14 of a 28-day cycle.

questions.question: The placenta functions to:
options: Supply the fetus with nutrients and O₂, remove waste
questions.answer: Supply the fetus with nutrients and O₂, remove waste
questions.explanation: The placenta allows exchange between maternal and fetal blood — O₂ and nutrients enter fetal blood; CO₂ and wastes leave.

questions.question: Which hormone triggers ovulation?
questions.explanation: A surge in LH (luteinising hormone) around day 14 triggers the follicle to rupture and release the egg — ovulation.

questions.question: Progesterone in the luteal phase maintains:
questions.explanation: Progesterone (from corpus luteum) maintains the thickened uterine lining, ready for implantation. If no fertilisation, progesterone falls and menstruation begins.

questions.question: What is the zygote?
questions.explanation: A zygote is the diploid cell formed when a sperm (n) fuses with an egg (n) during fertilisation. It contains 46 chromosomes.

questions.question: Condoms are the only contraception that also:
questions.explanation: Condoms provide a physical barrier against both pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections (STIs).

questions.question: HIV primarily attacks which cells?
questions.explanation: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) specifically targets and destroys CD4 T-helper cells, progressively weakening the immune system.

questions.question: Sperm are produced in the:
questions.explanation: Sperm are produced in the testes (by meiosis in seminiferous tubules). They then mature in the epididymis.

questions.question: What does FSH do in the female cycle?
questions.explanation: FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone) from the pituitary gland stimulates a follicle in the ovary to mature and produce oestrogen.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

lessons.title: The Nervous System and Reflex Arcs

content.body: The nervous system provides fast, short-lived coordination. It consists of the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and the peripheral nervous system (sensory and motor neurons).

Nerve impulse pathway:
Sense organ (receptor) → sensory neuron → CNS → motor neuron → effector (muscle/gland)

Synapse: junction between neurons
→ Electrical impulse → neurotransmitter release (e.g. acetylcholine)
→ Crosses synapse → new impulse in next neuron

content.title: Reflex Arc (involuntary, rapid)
content.body:
Stimulus → receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (in spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector → response

Example: touching hot object
Receptor in finger → sensory neuron → spinal cord relay → motor neuron → bicep contracts → hand pulls away

Reflex advantages: faster than voluntary (no brain processing), protects body from damage

content.title: Hormones vs Nervous System
content.body:
Hormones:
• Chemical messengers in blood
• Slow, long-lasting effect
• Widespread target
• Examples: insulin, adrenaline

Nervous:
• Electrical signals in neurons
• Fast, short-lasting
• Specific target
• Examples: reflexes, conscious actions

questions.question: The central nervous system consists of...
questions.explanation: The CNS (Central Nervous System) comprises the brain and spinal cord. Peripheral nervous system is everything else.

questions.question: The sequence in a reflex arc is...
questions.explanation: Reflex arc: stimulus → receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector (muscle/gland).

questions.question: Reflex actions are faster than voluntary actions because...
options: The signal bypasses the brain (processed in spinal cord)
questions.answer: The signal bypasses the brain (processed in spinal cord)
questions.explanation: Reflexes are processed in the spinal cord, not brain — shorter pathway = faster response time = better protection.

questions.question: A synapse is...
questions.explanation: A synapse is the tiny gap between neurons. Signal crosses by chemical neurotransmitters (not electrical).

questions.question: Neurotransmitters cross the synapse by...
options: Diffusion of chemical molecules across the cleft
questions.answer: Diffusion of chemical molecules across the cleft
questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters (e.g. acetylcholine) are released from vesicles and diffuse across the synaptic cleft to bind receptors on the next neuron.

questions.question: Compared to hormones, nervous responses are...
questions.explanation: Nerve impulses travel at ~100 m/s and go to specific targets. Hormones travel in blood (slower) and can affect many organs.

questions.question: The effector in a reflex arc is...
options: A muscle or gland that produces the response
questions.answer: A muscle or gland that produces the response
questions.explanation: Effectors are the organs that respond to nerve signals — muscles contract and glands secrete.

questions.question: Myelination of neurons...
questions.explanation: Myelin sheath (fatty insulation) causes saltatory conduction — impulse 'jumps' between nodes → much faster transmission.

questions.question: The pupil reflex (constricting in bright light) protects...
options: The retina from damage by too much light
questions.answer: The retina from damage by too much light
questions.explanation: Bright light triggers pupil constriction (iris muscles) — reduces light entering eye to protect sensitive retinal cells.

questions.question: Multiple sclerosis destroys myelin. This causes...
options: Slowed or blocked nerve impulse transmission
questions.answer: Slowed or blocked nerve impulse transmission
questions.explanation: Without myelin, saltatory conduction fails. Impulses are slower, weaker or blocked → movement and sensation problems.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Homeostasis & Hormones. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Homeostasis & Hormones concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Homeostasis & Hormones builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Homeostasis & Hormones. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Homeostasis & Hormones. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Homeostasis & Hormones include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Homeostasis & Hormones. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Homeostasis & Hormones. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Homeostasis & Hormones concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Homeostasis & Hormones builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Homeostasis & Hormones. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Homeostasis & Hormones. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Homeostasis & Hormones include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Homeostasis & Hormones. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
